chapters.title: Lead Like Lives Depend On It

articles.title: Nair joins Fenergo executive team to scale global revenue - FinTech Global
articles.snippet: Nair joins Fenergo executive team to scale global revenue  FinTech Global

articles.title: Buffini & Company adds three senior executives to scale coaching - HousingWire
articles.snippet: Buffini & Company adds three senior executives to scale coaching  HousingWire

articles.title: AdvanCell Strengthens Executive Leadership to Accelerate U.S. Expansion and Scale its Global Targeted Alpha Therapy Platform - Business Wire
articles.snippet: AdvanCell Strengthens Executive Leadership to Accelerate U.S. Expansion and Scale its Global Targeted Alpha Therapy Platform  Business Wire

post1.text:
Most companies scaling past $50M don't have a strategy problem. They have a leadership density problem.

When Buffini & Company recently brought on three senior executives specifically to scale their coaching operations, they weren't just adding headcount. They were making a calculated bet that the right leaders, placed at the right level, are the actual growth mechanism.

Here's what that means for you:

1. Your ceiling as a founder is often your company's ceiling. If you're still the decision hub for everything, you haven't built a leadership team. You've built a dependency.

2. Scaling past $100M requires leaders who can own outcomes, not just manage tasks. That distinction is everything.

3. The executives you need at $150M are almost never the ones who got you to $75M. Loyalty is valuable. Fit for the next stage is non-negotiable.

The companies that stall between $50M and $200M usually aren't short on capital or customers. They're short on leaders who can carry real weight.

So ask yourself: if you stepped away for 90 days, would the business grow or shrink?

post2.text:
A recent piece in HousingWire caught our attention. Buffini & Company just added three senior executives specifically to scale their coaching business.

That decision deserves more credit than it will get.

Most companies in the $50M to $200M range wait too long to build out the executive layer. They promote high performers who aren't ready, or they underpay for outside talent and wonder why the results are inconsistent.

At Autobahn, we see this constantly. The leaders who built the first $50M are rarely equipped, without significant development, to architect the next $200M. Bringing in experienced executives before you desperately need them is a power move, not an expense.

If your growth has plateaued, look at your org chart before you look at your marketing budget.

Read the full article here: https://news.google.com/rss/articles/CBMicEFVX3lxTE80SmJLNXdFS1VDcE9QNXQ2YzNFUDNUSzItcWljNWEyb2NEUVJ0UzdweEIzaVZyclAyWk5fcW54Nlo2U21GSVVoMHFJaVpLSFdaMU1tMXRIWU8wMTJvc3RJNTM5VkhoaVFxZnZFM0VlWVU

post3.text:
When AdvanCell recently strengthened its executive leadership team to accelerate U.S. expansion, they were doing something most scaling companies do far too late: treating leadership structure as a growth strategy, not an afterthought.

Chapter 1 of our upcoming book, Rock Your Business, is titled Lead Like Lives Depend On It. Written by Katherine Slain, it opens with a premise that makes most executives uncomfortable. The leadership behaviors that feel natural at $50M are often the exact behaviors that prevent you from reaching $500M.

Katherine doesn't just diagnose the problem. She gives scaling leaders a concrete way to evaluate whether they are built for the road ahead, or quietly becoming the bottleneck.

One insight that will stay with you: there is a difference between leading a company and leading a company that can survive you.

Rock Your Business, available soon. Follow us for updates.

posts.blog_hook: The most expensive leadership mistake scaling companies make isn't hiring the wrong person. It's keeping the right person in the wrong role for too long. Between $50M and $500M, leadership architecture is the growth lever that most executives ignore until it is too late. Here is what companies getting it right are doing differently.
posts.email_subject: Your leadership team is your speed limit

chapters.title: Maximize Your Multiple to Achieve Your Exit

articles.title: How to Create an Exit Strategy for Your Business - U.S. Chamber of Commerce
articles.snippet: How to Create an Exit Strategy for Your Business  U.S. Chamber of Commerce

articles.title: Business Exit Strategies for Startup Founders & Entrepreneurs - J.P. Morgan
articles.snippet: Business Exit Strategies for Startup Founders & Entrepreneurs  J.P. Morgan

articles.title: Why Boomer Business Owners Need to Set Exit Strategies Now - inc.com
articles.snippet: Why Boomer Business Owners Need to Set Exit Strategies Now  inc.com

post1.text:
Most business owners think about their exit too late. By the time they're ready to sell, they've already left millions on the table.

The uncomfortable truth: your multiple is built years before you ever talk to a buyer. Waiting until you're emotionally ready to exit is one of the most expensive mistakes a founder can make. Inc. recently highlighted how an entire generation of business owners is approaching exit readiness without the preparation their companies actually require. The gap between what they expect to receive and what buyers will pay is significant.

Here's what $50M+ leaders should be thinking about right now:

1. Buyers pay premiums for predictability. Recurring revenue, documented processes, and leadership depth matter more than last year's EBITDA.

2. Customer concentration is a silent valuation killer. If one client represents more than 20% of revenue, expect scrutiny and a discount.

3. Your exit multiple is not determined at the negotiating table. It's determined by the decisions you make in the three to five years before you sit down.

What would your business actually be worth to a buyer today, and are you prepared for that answer?

post2.text:
A recent piece in Inc. makes a compelling case that Baby Boomer business owners are running out of time to build proper exit strategies, and the numbers behind that argument are hard to ignore.

At Autobahn, we see this pattern constantly with companies in the $50M to $500M range. Owners who built great businesses often have no structured plan for what happens next. That absence of planning does not just affect the owner. It affects buyers, employees, and the long-term value of everything they built.

Scaling companies need to treat their exit as a strategic destination, not a last-minute transaction. The earlier you architect it, the more leverage you have.

If you are leading a growth-stage company and have not mapped your exit runway, now is the time to start. Read the full article here: https://news.google.com/rss/articles/CBMiowFBVV95cUxPbTk4M2VFV3VIdEE5SVIyNDVsOUpUQUlUZGlUY0dLMnd1eF9QUEdqV2EtbG1HVHJiQW51N3FZenE4Qnl4OUZfcnk2SkVETzQ1UUpkYWV5TFpjeEpqLV9keTl3M0FLUDNLT3pGdmU0WVl1QTVibGtfVkxuY2p3MnJZbXdtWGZhRTJBRHh6WTR4MzZkQ2R2Xzh5cGx3bUl6U0dwX2dB?oc=5

post3.text:
Exit planning is not an end-of-career conversation. It is a growth strategy.

Chapter 2 of Rock Your Business, titled 'Maximize Your Multiple to Achieve Your Exit,' tackles exactly this. Written by Paul Belair and George Hohman, the chapter reframes how scaling company leaders should think about valuation. It is not just about what your business earns. It is about what a buyer believes it will earn without you in the room.

That distinction changes everything about how you build, hire, and operate between now and the day you decide to sell.

One insight worth sitting with: the decisions that drive your multiple are operational decisions, not financial ones. The financials are just the scoreboard.

Rock Your Business, available soon. Follow us for updates.

posts.blog_hook: Most business owners set their exit strategy the same way they set a New Year's resolution: with good intentions and no real structure. By the time they're serious about selling, the leverage is already gone. The multiple you receive is not negotiated at closing. It is built, quietly and deliberately, in the years before you ever take a call from a buyer.
posts.email_subject: Your exit multiple is already being set

chapters.title: What We Learned Selling Fish Food and Nike Shoes

articles.title: Wrike Now Offers a Strategic Portfolio Management Solution to Bridge Strategy and Execution, Enabling Organizations to Plan and Deliver True Transformation - Business Wire
articles.snippet: Wrike Now Offers a Strategic Portfolio Management Solution to Bridge Strategy and Execution, Enabling Organizations to Plan and Deliver True Transformation  Business Wire

articles.title: From paper to performance: How to execute your 2026 credit union strategic plan - CUInsight
articles.snippet: From paper to performance: How to execute your 2026 credit union strategic plan  CUInsight

articles.title: Why Leadership Teams Fail At Strategy Execution And How To Fix It - Forbes
articles.snippet: Why Leadership Teams Fail At Strategy Execution And How To Fix It  Forbes

post1.text:
Most leadership teams don't fail at strategy. They fail at the moment strategy meets reality.

Forbes recently examined why execution breaks down even when the plan looks solid on paper. The finding is uncomfortable: the plan is rarely the problem. The people accountable for carrying it out are operating without clear ownership, timelines, or feedback loops.

Here is what that means for companies in the $50M to $500M range:

1. A strategy deck is not a strategy. If your leadership team cannot recite the top three priorities without looking at a slide, the plan is already dead.

2. Accountability gaps compound fast at scale. What one person can track in their head at $10M requires explicit ownership structures by the time you hit $75M.

3. Quarterly check-ins are too infrequent. Companies that close the strategy-execution gap review progress monthly at minimum, and they do it with specificity, not generalities.

The hard question for your next leadership meeting: If you removed yourself from the room, would your team execute the strategy the same way?

post2.text:
A recent piece in Forbes broke down exactly why leadership teams fall short when it comes to executing their own strategies. The core issue: most teams confuse alignment in the conference room with alignment in the field.

At Autobahn, we see this constantly with companies scaling past $50M. The strategy was built by smart people. It was debated, refined, and approved. Then it sat.

The bridge between a great plan and real results is not a better framework. It is disciplined, repeated follow-through with accountability tied to specific names and specific dates. That discipline has to be built into how your leadership team operates every single week, not just at annual planning.

If your strategy is gathering dust, this article is worth your time.

https://news.google.com/rss/articles/CBMiugFBVV95cUxOSXpNczJQdVBKTWlEV0dsby1FaEFGUVg5X1B3Zk9qUGI2bDJMSUxydGhRdURFUUdERXhBRmNzUndTVXhITTREMzMxdk9faE5rUldabk9ld2p2dHdfeXJjVEpMckJ6dFZNckFkTkk5ZmJ5MHRVSm5iSXo2WGlwc0NoeTBvcUYxTF9pbUNmaDB4LUdlNk9pMll0Y01BRTNVYzJObzRrV3pCN19scHYtdThYcHJVUVFYbjFMbkE

post3.text:
The Forbes piece on strategy execution failures hits close to home for anyone who has tried to scale a business past $50M. Getting leadership aligned on a plan is hard. Keeping them accountable to it while the business keeps moving is harder.

Chapter 3 of Rock Your Business, titled "What We Learned Selling Fish Food and Nike Shoes," tackles this head-on. Co-author Rafa Loyo draws from real operational experience across very different industries to make a point that most strategy consultants miss: the industry almost never determines whether execution succeeds. The internal rhythm of the company does.

What that rhythm looks like, and how to build it intentionally as you scale, is something Rafa and co-author Jim Randall lay out with refreshing specificity.

Rock Your Business — available soon. Follow us for updates.

posts.blog_hook: Most companies between $50M and $500M have a strategy. Very few have an execution system. The gap between those two things is where growth stalls, leadership trust erodes, and competitors quietly close the distance. If your team built a plan last quarter and cannot tell you its current status without pulling up a spreadsheet, you do not have a strategy problem. You have a discipline problem.
posts.email_subject: Your strategy isn't broken. Your execution is.

chapters.title: Every Day Without a Real Sales Strategy is a Donation

articles.title: AI Time Savings Won’t Drive Revenue Unless CSOs Intervene - Gartner
articles.snippet: AI Time Savings Won’t Drive Revenue Unless CSOs Intervene  Gartner

articles.title: B2B Sales Strategy Now Demands AI, Personalization to Survive - abasto.com
articles.snippet: B2B Sales Strategy Now Demands AI, Personalization to Survive  abasto.com

articles.title: How to Double B2B Sales Growth - Bain & Company
articles.snippet: How to Double B2B Sales Growth  Bain & Company

post1.text:
Most companies at $50M don't have a sales strategy. They have a sales habit.

There's a difference. A habit is what your team does because it worked before. A strategy is a deliberate set of choices about who you target, how you win, and what you stop doing.

Bain & Company recently published research showing that doubling B2B sales growth comes down to a handful of specific, repeatable behaviors, not heroic reps or a bigger headcount. The companies that scale are the ones that build the system, not just the team.

Three things leaders at your stage get wrong:

1. They confuse revenue growth with a working sales strategy.
2. They let their best reps define the process instead of the other way around.
3. They measure activity instead of pipeline quality.

If your top rep left tomorrow, would your sales engine still run?

That answer tells you everything about where your strategy actually stands.

post2.text:
A recent piece from Gartner makes a point that every Chief Sales Officer at a scaling company needs to hear: AI time savings do not automatically convert into revenue. Without deliberate intervention from sales leadership, that recovered time gets absorbed into the organization without moving the number.

This is exactly the trap we see companies fall into between $50M and $500M. New tools get adopted, efficiency goes up on paper, but revenue growth stays flat. The missing piece is always strategic intentionality at the top.

Saving your reps two hours a week matters only if leadership has defined what those two hours should be spent on.

If your sales leadership isn't actively shaping how AI-recovered time gets redirected toward revenue-generating activity, you're funding a productivity experiment, not a growth strategy.

Read the full Gartner findings here: https://news.google.com/rss/articles/CBMiYEFVX3lxTE83YmJZYVNxUUtqV1lJanRha3hGWnFtdjJYSm5FWURzTUFrVm1uTDB2R1VRWklWVlM4cVhURDNTMVFxVmw0Ql9YZUZrdndhcC1JWFRZczVJRmxlR1REQmVvMg?oc=5

post3.text:
A recent report from abasto.com put it plainly: B2B sales strategy now requires AI and personalization just to survive. That's a significant shift from even three years ago.

But survival isn't the goal. Scale is.

Chapter 4 of our upcoming book, Rock Your Business, is titled 'Every Day Without a Real Sales Strategy is a Donation.' Written by Deb and Brandon, this chapter confronts something most $50M companies don't want to admit: the approach that got you here is quietly costing you the growth you're chasing.

One thing Deb and Brandon make clear is that a real sales strategy has a specific structure that most leadership teams have never actually built. Knowing that gap exists is the first step.

Rock Your Business, available soon. Follow us for updates.

posts.blog_hook: If your company crossed $50M on gut instinct and great relationships, congratulations. Now those same things are slowing you down. The research is clear: scaling B2B revenue past that threshold requires a sales strategy that runs without your best rep in the room. Here's what that actually looks like and why most leadership teams have never built one.
posts.email_subject: Your sales habit isn't a strategy

chapters.title: What Does Your Marketing Smell Like?

articles.title: India's creative economy is booming, but cultural infrastructure must catch up: BMW's Thomas Girst - Storyboard18
articles.snippet: India's creative economy is booming, but cultural infrastructure must catch up: BMW's Thomas Girst  Storyboard18

articles.title: Why JBL is building marketing around culture not campaigns - Brand Innovators
articles.snippet: Why JBL is building marketing around culture not campaigns  Brand Innovators

articles.title: Big Pharma’s Big Brand: Inside Eli Lilly’s marketing culture - Fierce Pharma
articles.snippet: Big Pharma’s Big Brand: Inside Eli Lilly’s marketing culture  Fierce Pharma

post1.text:
Your brand is not your logo. It is not your tagline. It is the feeling people get when they walk away from any interaction with your company.

JBL figured this out. Rather than pouring budget into campaigns that disappear after the media buy ends, they built their marketing around cultural moments that people actually care about. The result is brand equity that compounds instead of evaporates.

For leaders running $50M to $500M businesses, this distinction matters enormously:

1. Campaigns rent attention. Culture owns it. The companies scaling fastest are investing in communities and moments, not just media placements.

2. Your brand has a smell. Customers can tell when your marketing is disconnected from how your company actually operates. Consistency between what you say and what you do is not optional at scale.

3. Marketing built around culture is harder to copy than a campaign. It becomes a genuine competitive moat.

The leaders who treat brand as a strategic asset, not a line item, are the ones who make it to $500M.

What does your marketing actually feel like to the people on the receiving end?

post2.text:
A recent piece in Brand Innovators caught our attention. It breaks down how JBL has deliberately shifted its marketing strategy away from traditional campaigns and toward building around culture. Not seasonal promotions. Not product launches. Culture.

For companies in the $50M to $500M growth range, this is worth sitting with. At that stage, most marketing budgets are still chasing short-term conversion. But the brands that win at scale build meaning before they need it.

At Autobahn, we see this pattern consistently. The businesses that struggle to grow past a certain revenue ceiling often have marketing that looks outward but does not reflect what is actually happening inside the company.

Culture-led marketing starts internally. If you cannot describe what your brand stands for without using the word 'solutions,' it is time to reassess.

Read the full piece here: https://news.google.com/rss/articles/CBMikgFBVV95cUxPbXpYamE0dkF6MWVkcEkwUHlnSHk5bUtIbmV4ZTlDNEk3MEpURWZDdUo5b1Q4aTMtZkIzUy1scXU3VIJUREY2Vks0NjFGV0s2QXYtejBPMm93NlRrSC00MHRSdlpQcnJqeW00akp4eFRLeTZwRGxOV1BEQmR0eEpoVXB0cFpPaUhVeTBtU3E3WGxfZw

post3.text:
JBL building its marketing around culture instead of campaigns is not a trend. It is a signal about where serious brand strategy is heading.

Chapter 5 of our upcoming book, 'Rock Your Business: Navigating the Road from $50 Million to $500 Million and Beyond,' takes this head-on. The chapter is titled 'What Does Your Marketing Smell Like?' and it was written by Kayli Simpson and Yasmin West.

The premise is simple but the implications are not. By the time your company reaches $50M, your brand has already developed a scent. Customers, employees, and partners are picking it up whether you intended it or not. The question is whether you are managing it deliberately.

Kayli and Yasmin lay out a framework for mid-market leaders who want to take real ownership of how their brand is perceived at scale, not just what it says.

Rock Your Business, available soon. Follow us for updates.

posts.blog_hook: Most growing companies treat marketing as a megaphone and wonder why no one is listening. By the time you hit $50M in revenue, your brand already has a reputation. The real question is whether you built it on purpose. JBL's pivot toward culture-led marketing is a lesson in what separates brands that scale from brands that plateau. Here is what mid-market leaders need to understand before their next campaign budget gets approved.
posts.email_subject: What does your brand actually smell like?

chapters.title: The Talent Engine
chapters.authors: Caleb Hawkins, Darla Klein & Yasmin West

articles.title: Precision Over Scale: The New Rules of Hiring in 2026 - SHRM
articles.snippet: Precision Over Scale: The New Rules of Hiring in 2026  SHRM

articles.title: WorkWave Names Melinda Vedder as Chief Human Resources Officer - Pest Control Technology
articles.snippet: WorkWave Names Melinda Vedder as Chief Human Resources Officer  Pest Control Technology

articles.title: Jobsity Announces Strategic Acquisition of Eureka Labs, Expanding Scale and Strengthening Its Position as a Leading Nearshore Talent Partner - Business Wire
articles.snippet: Jobsity Announces Strategic Acquisition of Eureka Labs, Expanding Scale and Strengthening Its Position as a Leading Nearshore Talent Partner  Business Wire

post1.text:
Hiring more people is not a growth strategy. It is a cost strategy masquerading as one.

SHRM recently reported that companies are shifting away from volume-based hiring toward precision-based hiring, focusing on fewer, higher-impact roles rather than scaling headcount as a default response to growth pressure.

For leaders scaling past $50M, this reframe is critical. Here is what it means in practice:

First, your next hire should solve a specific constraint, not a general capacity problem. If you cannot name the bottleneck the role removes, you are not ready to post the job.

Second, the quality of your hiring process is a direct signal of your operational maturity. Rushed hiring at this stage is one of the fastest ways to stall momentum.

Third, precision hiring requires knowing your org structure 12 to 18 months out, not just today. Most companies at this stage are reacting instead of designing.

Growth does not come from adding people. It comes from adding the right people at the right moment.

What is driving your next hire, strategy or urgency?

post2.text:
A recent piece in SHRM makes a strong case that precision is replacing scale as the defining principle of effective hiring in 2026. Companies are rethinking the default assumption that more headcount equals more capability.

At Autobahn, we see this pattern constantly with companies scaling from $50M toward $500M. The leaders who grow fastest are not the ones hiring the most. They are the ones who have built a repeatable system for identifying exactly which role, at exactly which moment, will unlock the next stage of growth.

If your talent decisions are still reactive, you are leaving performance on the table.

Read the SHRM piece and ask yourself: does your hiring process reflect a strategy or a habit?

https://news.google.com/rss/articles/CBMioAFBVV95cUxPRFVYR0VscXJpMmVqV2dwazJDTkstVDJTNG9uTlZlWHJPVkFqQ0MzZTRQSUYwUDZweWw2dE52THZKSnhBTzQtb1g5UUYzbmJTbjFJcXlVYno2ZzU4aE0yNVRwN2RIN05xTWJVWnZQejVyY2xRNmJ2SF83QmVHel9GYmdWTEdEMWZLMVk5OThMNTF0TmdKR1F4bG84cnZRZ0FU?oc=5

post3.text:
SHRM is reporting that precision hiring is replacing volume hiring as the standard for high-performing companies. This is not a trend. For scaling businesses, it is a survival skill.

Chapter 6 of Rock Your Business, titled The Talent Engine, tackles exactly this challenge. Authors Caleb Hawkins, Darla Klein, and Yasmin West lay out what separates companies that build genuine talent momentum from those that just keep adding headcount and wondering why growth stalls.

One thing they make clear: at the $50M to $500M stage, your people decisions compound faster than almost any other operational choice. The framework they share gives leaders a way to evaluate talent needs before the pressure hits, not after.

If your team is your growth engine, you need to know how to build it intentionally.

Rock Your Business -- available soon. Follow us for updates.

posts.blog_hook: Most companies scaling past $50M treat hiring as a volume problem. The faster you grow, the more people you add. But SHRM's latest research and a growing body of evidence from high-growth firms tells a different story. Precision beats scale. The leaders winning at this stage are not hiring more. They are hiring smarter, earlier, and with a clear view of where the organization needs to be 18 months from now.
posts.email_subject: Stop hiring for growth. Start hiring for it.

chapters.title: Death by KPIs
chapters.authors: Héctor Arias, Matt Radicelli & Jim Randall

articles.title: 11 Key Hospitality KPIs to Track - Oracle NetSuite
articles.snippet: 11 Key Hospitality KPIs to Track  Oracle NetSuite

articles.title: The KPIs to Help Manufacturers Improve Company Performance - CLA (CliftonLarsonAllen)
articles.snippet: The KPIs to Help Manufacturers Improve Company Performance  CLA (CliftonLarsonAllen)

articles.title: 14 Tools to Track KPIs for Your Business - Business.com
articles.snippet: 14 Tools to Track KPIs for Your Business  Business.com

post1.text:
More KPIs will not make your company perform better. In most cases, they do the opposite.

Oracle NetSuite recently broke down the metrics hospitality businesses should track. The list was useful. But the real risk for scaling companies is not having too few metrics. It is having so many that your leadership team stops acting on any of them.

Here is what we see in companies between $50M and $500M:

1. Leaders confuse activity metrics with outcome metrics. Tracking what your team does is not the same as tracking what it produces.

2. KPI dashboards become reporting rituals instead of decision tools. If your weekly meeting reviews 40 numbers but changes nothing, the dashboard is theater.

3. The metrics that matter most are usually the ones nobody wants to own. Accountability gaps live inside KPI blind spots.

Growth companies do not need more visibility. They need fewer, sharper numbers tied to real consequences.

What is the one metric your leadership team tracks but consistently fails to act on?

post2.text:
A recent piece in CLA (CliftonLarsonAllen) outlines the KPIs manufacturers should use to sharpen company performance. It is a solid operational breakdown for teams trying to get a handle on their numbers.

But here is what the article does not address: at scale, the KPI problem is rarely about selection. It is about alignment.

When a manufacturer crosses $50M, the metrics that worked at $20M start to create noise instead of clarity. Different departments optimize for their own numbers. The company moves in multiple directions at once and calls it growth.

The companies that scale cleanly do one thing differently. They treat KPIs as a leadership alignment tool, not just a reporting function.

If your metrics are not driving unified decisions across your executive team, read this and think about what is missing.

https://news.google.com/rss/articles/CBMiugFBVV95cUxNc3hGVVVxVnMwVEpLcmxaNWJzaURJbERjczZaSDhlcnhvOFlHX1VZU0tETkctQjFqRVhZVkNtcFNoUWtfSmRhSmU5N3pYc25IVzlfTEs5TjFZWk1RTmhZd2JzSkpPSXVmQndRRHRBM29CaDBoWnVWRXcxMnBhWkcwT3dmWTAwSDFSeS1hYzFtRUNtd05nWE9PX25nbzRfc2ZzSjVFVURMY0hMRkdVMmtOdjVOYUluUXJIcEE

post3.text:
Business.com recently listed 14 tools companies can use to track KPIs. The tools are real. The problem they solve, though, is only half the battle.

Chapter 7 of our upcoming book, Rock Your Business, is titled Death by KPIs. Co-authored by Héctor Arias, Matt Radicelli, and Jim Randall, it tackles something most scaling companies never want to admit: the metrics you have built can be the very thing holding your growth back.

One insight the chapter surfaces is that most leadership teams are not suffering from a lack of data. They are suffering from a lack of agreement on what the data means and who is responsible when the numbers move the wrong way.

Tracking KPIs is easy. Building a culture where they drive action is not.

Rock Your Business, available soon. Follow us for updates.

posts.blog_hook: Most scaling companies do not have a data problem. They have a discipline problem. By the time a business hits $50M, it has more metrics than it knows what to do with and fewer clear owners than it needs. Death by KPIs is real, and it is not caused by ignorance. It is caused by the quiet organizational habits that turn dashboards into wallpaper.
posts.email_subject: Your KPIs might be slowing you down

articles.title: Performance Management Needs New Metrics in the AI Era - Harvard Business Review
articles.snippet: Performance Management Needs New Metrics in the AI Era  Harvard Business Review

articles.title: ‘Talk to My A.I. Twin’: Busy Executives Have a New Productivity Hack - The New York Times
articles.snippet: ‘Talk to My A.I. Twin’: Busy Executives Have a New Productivity Hack  The New York Times

articles.title: Thousands of CEOs admit AI had no impact on employment or productivity—and it has economists resurrecting a paradox from 40 years ago - Fortune
articles.snippet: Thousands of CEOs admit AI had no impact on employment or productivity—and it has economists resurrecting a paradox from 40 years ago  Fortune

post1.text:
Most companies are using AI wrong, and their productivity numbers prove it.

Fortune recently reported that thousands of CEOs acknowledge AI has produced no measurable impact on employment or productivity. Economists are now revisiting a 40-year-old paradox to explain why. This is not a technology problem. It is a leadership problem.

Here is what scaling companies need to understand:

First, AI tools without process redesign are just expensive shortcuts. Dropping a new tool into a broken workflow speeds up the dysfunction.

Second, the metrics you use to evaluate performance were built for a pre-AI world. If your KPIs have not changed, your expectations are misaligned with reality.

Third, the companies seeing real gains are not using AI to replace work. They are using it to elevate the quality of decisions made by their existing teams.

If your AI investments are not moving your core numbers, the problem is not the technology you chose.

What would you have to change about your operations before AI could actually work?

post2.text:
A recent piece in The New York Times explored how busy executives are now deploying AI versions of themselves to handle meetings, answer questions, and extend their availability beyond the workday.

This is worth paying attention to if you are running a company between $50M and $500M.

At that stage, the bottleneck is almost always the leader. Decisions stall because access to the right person is limited. An AI twin does not solve your strategy. But it can dissolve the access problem that slows everything else down.

At Autobahn, we work with leaders who are already stretched thin. The question is never whether to use a tool. The question is whether using it makes your organization faster and smarter, or just more complicated.

Read the full article and think about where your own access bottleneck is hiding.

https://news.google.com/rss/articles/CBMifkFVX3lxTE1fZnBYemx0U1pIYUJQSk1WSm1sdThzLWNxcnJmaUNqMDFSajVISUtSXzJBTWFtSW9PY0JjcUVZSjRSaTZwblhuYWg3NUM1Xy1MVmxpSWtHSUJzRmlGZjA1VHBHczEwbkl5UmswVW1qVi00Q0Z3NUtZeHZDb0lEQQ

post3.text:
Harvard Business Review is making the case that performance management needs entirely new metrics in the AI era. We agree, and we saw this coming.

Chapter 8 of our upcoming book, Rock Your Business, is called The Third Monitor Theory. It was written by Hudson Shank and Jonathan Slain, and it tackles exactly this challenge for companies scaling past $50M.

The chapter introduces a framework for how leaders should be structuring their relationship with AI at the operational level. Not as a productivity gimmick, and not as a future investment. As a core part of how decisions get made today.

One thing the chapter makes clear: the leaders who will win are not the ones who adopt AI fastest. They are the ones who build the right context around it.

There is a specific and practical way to do that. We will let the book explain the rest.

Rock Your Business, available soon. Follow us for updates.

posts.blog_hook: Thousands of CEOs just admitted AI has done nothing for their productivity. That is not a technology failure. That is a leadership and systems failure. The companies scaling past $50M and toward $500M are not waiting for better tools. They are building better operating structures around the tools they already have. Here is what that actually looks like.
posts.email_subject: Why AI isn't working for most CEOs

chapters.title: How Could You Be Successful and Not Have It?
chapters.authors: Samantha Frost, Caleb Hawkins & Rafa Loyo

articles.title: Why impostor syndrome follows high achievers all the way to the top - Forbes Australia
articles.snippet: Why impostor syndrome follows high achievers all the way to the top  Forbes Australia

articles.title: Don’t call it imposter syndrome - Fast Company
articles.snippet: Don’t call it imposter syndrome  Fast Company

articles.title: Alumni Careers: Understanding imposter syndrome and career growth - Imperial College London
articles.snippet: Alumni Careers: Understanding imposter syndrome and career growth  Imperial College London

post1.text:
The leaders most qualified to run a $100M+ company are often the ones most convinced they don't belong there.

That's not a flaw. It's a pattern.

Forbes recently reported that imposter syndrome doesn't fade as leaders climb higher. It scales with them. The bigger the role, the louder the internal doubt.

Here's what that means for executives navigating serious growth:

First, self-doubt at the top is not a signal to slow down. It's often a signal that the stakes are real and you're paying attention.

Second, leaders who never question their own judgment are frequently the dangerous ones. A calibrated sense of uncertainty keeps decision-making honest.

Third, the problem isn't the feeling. The problem is when leaders let it drive avoidance. Delayed hiring decisions, missed acquisitions, shrinking from bold strategy. That's where imposter syndrome actually costs money.

The question worth sitting with: Are your growth decisions being made from your actual capabilities, or from the version of yourself you haven't fully accepted yet?

post2.text:
A recent piece in Fast Company made a sharp argument: we should stop calling it imposter syndrome altogether.

The label, the article suggests, pathologizes something that is often a rational response to real pressure and high stakes.

For companies scaling past $50M, this reframe matters more than it might seem.

When leaders at growing companies believe something is psychologically wrong with them, they hide it. They stop asking for help. They avoid the kind of outside perspective that actually accelerates growth.

At Autobahn, we see this constantly. The executives who scale fastest are not the most confident ones in the room. They're the ones willing to name what they don't know and build around it.

Read the Fast Company piece and consider what you've been calling a personal problem that might actually be a leadership asset in disguise.

https://news.google.com/rss/articles/CBMidkFVX3lxTE9PRnBrU1NWWm1BeEl3dk5qVjFRc0M0T2ctZW9fbjdWd2NmdFlUZVVNeW9JV0VTWkktVmp0MlVjbFVEMXEweWZBS1d6bFBSMVNoNFVfWm5WaFo0aG1PT1RJN0p2Ylc0SkRONnBBcDYzZmNEUUJsRFE

post3.text:
Chapter 9 of Rock Your Business is titled "How Could You Be Successful and Not Have It?"

That title alone should stop any high-growth leader cold.

Authored by Samantha Frost, Caleb Hawkins, and Rafa Loyo, this chapter tackles something most business books are too polished to address: the psychological weight that comes with scaling a company to $50M, $100M, and beyond.

One insight the chapter surfaces is that imposter syndrome at scale is not about confidence. It's about identity. The leader who built a $50M company is not automatically the leader who can run a $500M one, and knowing that gap exists is actually the first sign you might be able to close it.

The chapter gives leaders a way to work through that tension without faking certainty they don't have.

Rock Your Business, available soon. Follow us for updates.

posts.blog_hook: The higher you climb, the louder the voice telling you that you don't belong there. Research confirms it scales with success, not against it. For leaders pushing companies past $50M, imposter syndrome isn't a personal problem to fix quietly. It's a force that shapes hiring decisions, strategic bets, and whether your company grows into what it's capable of becoming.
posts.email_subject: Why doubt follows your best leaders up

articles.title: The data says founders would do it again. Even in this economy. - Shopify
articles.snippet: The data says founders would do it again. Even in this economy.  Shopify

post1.text:
Work-life balance is a trap. The leaders scaling companies past $100M need life design, not balance.

Balance implies a scale where business and personal life compete. Design implies intention. Those are completely different games.

Shopify recently published data showing that founders would choose entrepreneurship again, even in a brutal economic environment. What drives that? It is not the money. It is the sense of authorship over their own story.

Here is what that means for leaders at your growth stage:

First, your personal goals have to be written down with the same rigor as your revenue targets. Vague intentions do not survive scaling pressure.

Second, time is not managed, it is allocated. Leaders who protect personal priorities like they protect key client relationships operate differently under stress.

Third, the version of you that gets the company to $500M has to be sustainable. Burning out at $200M is not a growth strategy.

What does success actually look like for you outside of the revenue number?

post2.text:
A recent piece from Shopify found that founders overwhelmingly say they would do it again, even now, even with everything the current economy is throwing at them.

That resilience is real. But at Autobahn, we see what happens when that same resilience becomes a liability. Leaders scaling from $50M to $500M often treat personal sacrifice as a competitive advantage. It is not. It is a deferred cost.

The founders who sustain that love for the work are the ones who built a life around the business, not just a business that consumed their life.

If you are scaling fast and running on fumes, that is worth a hard conversation. What structures are you putting in place now so that the version of you at $300M is still someone you recognize?

Read the full piece here: https://news.google.com/rss/articles/CBMiYkFVX3lxTFBZTUEtUXQ2MWtsQm5BY3hOaFNjTmxjUk5BRnJmWHhMM0tGMDhiTjZJcmhrNU1sQW1TamJsbjZtaUs5VVc0Z1hvWmZwZnNhUUFSRXhCM1EwR0QxcEV6d2xkRWZR?oc=5

post3.text:
Shopify data confirms what we hear from leaders every week: founders would choose this path again. But choosing it again and designing it better are two different things.

Chapter 10 of Rock Your Business, titled Coming Up for Air, tackles exactly this. Written by Samantha Frost alongside Jonathan and Katherine Slain, the chapter confronts something most business books ignore entirely: what do you actually want your life to look like at the finish line?

One insight that stops leaders cold is this: the plan to scale your company and the plan for your own life have to be written together, or one will eventually wreck the other.

This is not a chapter about self-care. It is a chapter about intention at scale.

Rock Your Business, available soon. Follow us for updates.

posts.blog_hook: Most leaders scaling past $50M have a growth plan, a hiring plan, and a capital plan. Almost none of them have a life plan. That gap does not show up immediately. It shows up at $200M when the person who built the company no longer recognizes themselves. Here is what intentional life design actually looks like at the executive level.
posts.email_subject: Your growth plan is missing one thing
